metadata.summary: Build AI research pipelines with a two-layer orchestrator: select the right tool for each stage, dispatch queries, normalize outputs, and validate coverage before synthesis.

content.title: AI Research Tools
content.summary: A two-layer agent structure for AI-driven market research pipelines: an orchestrator (Sonnet) selects the right tool for each research stage based on task type and available credentials, then dispatches sub-queries to stage-specific tools. The orchestrator merges outputs, flags gaps where no tool produced coverage, and signals a human checkpoint before synthesis. Each stage gets exactly one primary tool.

why.p: No single AI tool covers all market research stages. Assigning the same tool to multiple stages hides coverage gaps and produces biased, citation-poor results. Agents default to Claude or ChatGPT for all stages when not given explicit tool assignments. Tool outputs also have different schemas (Perplexity returns citation URLs; SerpAPI returns structured JSON; Google Trends returns DataFrames); the orchestrator must normalize before merging.

list.item: Building or auditing the tool stack for a market research pipeline
Deciding which AI tool handles which stage of a multi-step project
Replacing manual research workflows with agent-driven equivalents
Onboarding a new agent to an existing research pipeline
When a single tool already covers the full research scope
Proprietary or regulated research contexts requiring auditable, non-AI sources
When the research team lacks API credentials for candidate tools
One-off ad hoc queries where tool overhead exceeds research value

rule.statement: Use a two-layer agent structure: a Sonnet orchestrator that selects tools and generates stage queries, plus stage-specific tool calls (Perplexity, SerpAPI, Google Trends, Dovetail export). Never have the orchestrator make tool calls directly.
rule.rationale: Mixing selection logic and tool dispatch in one agent creates a monolithic prompt that is hard to debug when one stage fails. Separation allows stage-level retry without re-running the entire pipeline.

rule.statement: Assign exactly one primary tool per research stage. Do not allow the same tool to cover multiple stages.
rule.rationale: Using one tool for multiple stages hides coverage gaps. If Perplexity is used for both exploration and competitor intel, the competitor result will be citation-shallow because Perplexity is not optimized for tracking product and pricing changes.

list.item: exploration → Perplexity
competitor-intel → SerpAPI
trend → Google Trends (pytrends)
interview-analysis → Dovetail (export)
synthesis → Claude Sonnet

rule.statement: Before merging stage outputs in the synthesis step, normalize schemas: Perplexity returns citation URLs, SerpAPI returns structured JSON, Google Trends returns DataFrames. The orchestrator must convert all outputs to a common format before synthesis.
rule.rationale: Without normalization, the synthesis agent receives heterogeneous schemas and either truncates the unusual formats or produces inconsistent citations, breaking the source registry.

rule.statement: Verify tool credentials and rate limits at the start of each pipeline run. Failed tool calls silently missing from results is a common failure mode.
rule.rationale: A stage that fails due to an expired API key produces no output. Without explicit verification, the orchestrator treats missing output as "no data found" and continues, producing a synthesis with invisible gaps.

rule.statement: Run the tool stack on a known-answer test case before live research to validate API reliability.
rule.rationale: Validate tool behavior against a question with a known, stable answer (e.g., "What is the Anthropic API base URL?") before committing to a multi-stage research run. This catches misconfiguration before it contaminates real research.

antipattern.description: No single AI tool covers all stages; agents that try to use one tool for everything produce shallow, biased results.
antipattern.reason: Assign explicit tools per stage; do not let agents default to Claude or ChatGPT for all stages.

antipattern.description: Tool quality for market research degrades sharply outside English-language content.
antipattern.reason: Perplexity, SerpAPI, and Google Trends optimize for English signals; non-English research requires specialized regional tools.

antipattern.description: Competitor analysis tools (Crayon, Klue) require 2-4 weeks of initial setup before producing useful signals; agents cannot bootstrap this.
antipattern.reason: These tools require human configuration of competitor entities. Agents cannot invent the competitor list.

antipattern.description: Interview analysis tools (Looppanel, Dovetail) only operate on pre-uploaded transcripts — agents cannot conduct interviews.
antipattern.reason: These are transcript processors, not interview conductors. Transcripts must exist before the tool can be called.

antipattern.description: AI tools reflect the training cutoff or crawl date of their underlying data; freshness of results must be explicitly verified.
antipattern.reason: Perplexity crawls 2026 web data; older AI LLMs have 2024 cutoffs. Do not use tools without knowing their data freshness date.

list.item: pytrends — Google Trends trend signal (pip install pytrends)
serpapi — Structured search results (pip install google-search-results)
anthropic SDK — Orchestration and synthesis (pip install anthropic)
httpx — Async API calls to Perplexity, Contify (pip install httpx)
pandas — Tabulate and merge multi-tool outputs (pip install pandas)
Perplexity Pro — REST API, exploration and cited synthesis
ChatGPT API — REST API, general synthesis; weaker citations than Perplexity
Claude API — REST API, best for structured output and multi-step reasoning
Crayon — partial webhooks, competitor intel; manual setup required
Klue — partial, competitor intel; better NLP querying than Crayon
Contify — partial REST, competitor + market news
Brandwatch — partial REST, social sentiment; complex setup
Sprout Social — partial REST, social sentiment; more accessible API
Statista AI — export only, market data reports
CB Insights — partial export, company and funding data
Exploding Topics — REST API, emerging trend signals
Looppanel — partial export, interview analysis
Dovetail — partial export, interview and UX research synthesis

example.title: Multi-stage research pipeline prompt
example.code: <research_pipeline> <topic>AI research tools market, 2026</topic> <stages> <stage name="exploration" tool="perplexity">Broad landscape: who are the players, what are the use cases?</stage> <stage name="competitor-intel" tool="serpapi">Recent news, funding, product launches for top 5 players</stage> <stage name="trend" tool="google-trends">Search volume trends for key terms over 12 months</stage> <stage name="synthesis" tool="claude-sonnet">Merge stage outputs into a structured report with source registry</stage> </stages> <output>Markdown report, source registry, coverage gaps list</output> </research_pipeline>
example.note: Each stage declares its tool explicitly. Synthesis is a separate stage, not embedded in any tool call.

antipattern.description: Agents default to Claude or ChatGPT for all stages when not given explicit tool assignments.
antipattern.reason: General-purpose LLMs produce citation-poor results for competitive and market data stages. Stage-to-tool assignments must be explicit in the orchestrator prompt, not inferred.

antipattern.description: Rate limit errors from one tool propagate silently unless the orchestrator handles exceptions per tool.
antipattern.reason: Without per-tool exception handling, a rate-limited stage produces empty output that looks like "no data", corrupting the synthesis and producing gaps invisible to the human reviewer.

antipattern.description: Tool outputs have different schemas; the synthesis step receives inconsistent data unless normalized.
antipattern.reason: Perplexity → citations as URLs; SerpAPI → structured JSON; Google Trends → DataFrames. The orchestrator must normalize before the synthesis call, not after.

antipattern.description: "AI tool" in 2026 means both AI-native tools (Perplexity, Elicit) and legacy tools with AI features bolted on (Statista AI, SurveyMonkey AI); their reliability for agent pipelines differs significantly.
antipattern.reason: Always include the API reference in the agent's context for each tool. Agents will hallucinate capabilities for unfamiliar tools if given only the tool name.

template.purpose: Orchestrator that selects tool per stage and generates stage queries
XML prompt for multi-stage market research pipeline
